output:
# SLOTBLIND to SIGNAL: Exact-Collapse Descriptor Action Heads for Open-Catalog Autonomous Cyber Operations

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

CybORG frames autonomous cyber operations (ACO) as an adversarial decision-making problem for blue defenders and red attackers, with a common interface spanning simulation and emulation [1]. Its introduction makes the core motivation unusually explicit: cyber environments and action sets change as security research progresses, so static datasets and frozen action catalogs are a poor fit for ACO [1]. This sits inside the broader sim-to-real problem familiar from robotics, where policies trained cheaply in simulation must survive contact with higher-fidelity systems [20,21,22]. Cyber emulation testbeds such as DETER, Emulab, VINE, SmallWorld, and BRAWL address realism, reset, and scale from the infrastructure side [23,24,25,26,27].

The CAGE Challenge series has become the de facto benchmark path for CybORG-style ACO-RL agents [2,3,4,5]. In these scenarios, blue agents commonly learn with standard deep RL machinery such as PPO or DQN [13,14], mapping observations to a fixed softmax or Q-vector over a frozen catalog of K legal actions. Typical defensive actions include Monitor, Analyse, Remove, Restore, and DecoyServices instantiated over a fixed set of hosts or services. This architecture is operationally convenient, but it inherits the closed-action-set assumption of textbook RL [12].

Cyber operations, however, already have a structured language for action semantics. MITRE ATT&CK organizes adversary behavior into techniques, tactics, platforms, permissions, and effects, making many cyber actions describable beyond an arbitrary integer ID [6]. CTI extraction systems such as TTPDrill further show that technique-like structure can be mined from text and threat reports [31]. That makes cyber unusually well suited to descriptor-conditioned action selection, because new actions often arrive with names, ATT&CK mappings, affected services, privileges, preconditions, and expected effects.

Outside ACO, RL has studied large discrete action spaces, learned action representations, stochastic action sets, and lifelong learning under changing action sets [7,8,9,10,11]. Related language- or tool-conditioned systems choose among actions by conditioning on descriptions, plans, or tool affordances rather than only on fixed output indices [28,29,30]. These lines show that action descriptors can carry decision-relevant generalization. But they do not by themselves solve the CybORG/CAGE problem, because ACO also needs legal-action masking, adversarial dynamics, cyber-grounded descriptors, and safe adoption against existing fixed-head baselines.

The disconnect is therefore sharp: CybORG motivates ACO as open-world, adversarial, and nonstationary, yet deployed ACO policies often inherit a fixed-index action head from standard deep RL. The benchmark trains agents on a frozen catalog while the domain premise says the catalog will change. SLOTBLIND names this mismatch; SIGNAL is the minimal architectural correction.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

SLOTBLIND is a representational impossibility, not merely an inefficiency. A fixed-index CAGE action head computes one logit or Q-value per training-time slot, so its policy is a function over integer positions in a frozen catalog. If a new CVE response, ATT&CK subtechnique, host role, service, or red/blue action has no training-time slot, the policy cannot score, compare, or select it. The harm vanishes only in the degenerate closed-world case where deployment has exactly the same action catalog, host/service inventory, and TTP set as training.

This explains why the problem is silent under standard frozen-scenario mean reward. If evaluation rollouts never introduce out-of-catalog actions, the fixed head is never asked to represent the missing object. A CAGE agent can therefore score well on the original benchmark while being structurally unable to use a newly disclosed technique or newly available defensive action. That is exactly the failure mode anticipated by CybORG's warning that ACO action sets change and static datasets are inadequate [1].

Naive fixes do not remove the impossibility. Spare output slots only postpone failure and do not define semantics for an unseen action. Retraining from scratch can adapt after the fact, but it gives no zero-shot behavior and discards the operational value of a deployed baseline. Nearest-ID matching maps a new action to an old integer slot, but then the new action is not truly represented; it is forced to borrow another action's value even when its preconditions, privileges, or effects differ.

SIGNAL treats the action descriptor as a first-class input while leaving the rest of the CAGE agent intact. It replaces only the fixed action head with logit(a)=g(h,d_a), where h is the reused CAGE observation encoding and d_a contains a frozen sentence-transformer embedding of ATT&CK technique text plus structured one-hots for verb, host role/service, privilege, preconditions, expected effects, and an optional ID embedding. A bilinear query-key scorer, q=W_q h and k_a=W_k d_a, scores each per-step legal action under the existing legal-action mask. PPO, the value path, rollout machinery, and observation encoder are reused [13].

SIGNAL is adoption-safe because it has an exact-collapse identity. With beta=0, decisions route entirely through the original frozen CAGE K-action head, or equivalently a one-hot frozen descriptor lookup is initialized to reproduce the baseline logits over the original catalog. For every fixed seeded rollout state and legal-action mask, SIGNAL in collapse mode returns the same logits, action distribution or argmax, and downstream decision inputs as the baseline. The CI gate is E_collapse<=1e-6, where E_collapse is the maximum absolute legal-action logit gap across timesteps between baseline CAGE and collapsed SIGNAL.

The wedge is the join of four clauses: C1 open-catalog ACO, supporting K -> K+m expansion without resizing the network output; C2 ATT&CK-grounded descriptors, using cyber semantics rather than opaque action IDs; C3 zero-shot novel-technique evaluation, holding out action families, exploit families, host/service expansions, and red strategies; and C4 exact collapse to CAGE, giving decision identity on the frozen benchmark. Large-action RL covers parts of C1, action-representation RL covers parts of C2, stochastic/changing-action-set RL covers parts of C1 and C3, and fixed CAGE baselines cover C4 only by remaining closed-world [7,8,9,10,11]. The analytical claim is that the novelty lies in the C1-C4 join for CybORG/CAGE ACO, not in any single component alone.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can SIGNAL eliminate SLOTBLIND by enabling zero-shot selection of newly introduced MITRE ATT&CK-style actions in CybORG/CAGE while exactly collapsing to the fixed-head CAGE baseline on the original action catalog?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

ACO is an open-world operational problem, not a closed game. New CVEs, services, hosts, mitigations, and ATT&CK techniques appear continuously, and CybORG's own motivation is that cyber action sets change much faster than game AI or many robotics domains [1]. SLOTBLIND turns that reality into a concrete failure mode: a fixed K-way CAGE policy cannot even assign a score to a new defensive or adversarial action if it had no training-time slot. If SIGNAL can absorb K -> K+m actions by adding descriptors rather than resizing and retraining the policy head, it changes deployability: defenders could introduce a new patch, quarantine action, detection query, or ATT&CK-labeled response at disclosure time and immediately let the trained agent rank it.

The problem also goes to the heart of why CybORG/CAGE exists. CybORG argues that static datasets are inadequate because autonomous cyber behavior depends on a changing adversary and changing environment [1], and the CAGE Challenges operationalize that agenda as a benchmark series for autonomous cyber defense [2-5]. However, standard CAGE evaluations can still reward policies that are excellent inside one frozen catalog while being structurally incapable of catalog growth. Solving SLOTBLIND would therefore advance the ACO-RL agenda itself: future CAGE-style benchmarks could evaluate not just mean reward on a fixed scenario, but whether an agent can generalize across new CVEs, ATT&CK subtechniques, services, and response actions.

The methodological importance is broader than cyber. Variable and expanding action sets recur in recommendation, large discrete-action RL, robotics skill libraries, and tool-using agentic systems [7-11, 28-30]. SIGNAL is interesting because it makes the action descriptor first-class while preserving the rest of the CAGE pipeline: the observation encoder, RL loss, masks, rollout machinery, and baseline path remain intact. If it works in CybORG/CAGE, it supplies a template for other domains where new actions arrive as structured descriptions rather than as retrained output indices.

The exact-collapse requirement is especially important for safety and adoption. Security-critical defenders cannot accept a new learning architecture merely because it sometimes improves average reward; they need assurance that it does not regress on certified behavior. C4 gives that assurance: at beta=0, or with one-hot frozen descriptors and a baseline-initialized lookup, SIGNAL must reproduce the fixed-head CAGE logits, legal-action masked decisions, and value inputs term-for-term, with E_collapse <= 1e-6 on seeded rollouts. That makes SIGNAL an incremental, CI-gateable replacement for the action head rather than a wholesale replacement of the defender.

Finally, the proposal contributes an evaluation lens. Frozen-scenario mean reward, PPO score, or DQN score [13, 14] can hide catalog drift because the rollout never exposes out-of-catalog actions. Naming SLOTBLIND lets the community measure the missing axis directly: zero-shot held-out-technique performance, catalog-expansion regret, recovery time after new-action insertion, and no-regression collapse error on the original catalog. These measurements affect future research by making open-catalog robustness a first-class benchmark objective rather than an anecdotal deployment concern.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

O1 REPRESENTATIONAL BINDING IMPOSSIBILITY. A fixed CAGE PPO head scores integer slots, not actions. If a deployment action has no training-time slot, the policy cannot assign it a comparable logit, so it cannot be selected except by external hacks. Naive spare slots fail because the number and semantics of future hosts, services, CVEs, or TTPs are unknown, and unused slots receive no meaningful credit. SIGNAL addresses this by making logit(a) a function of a descriptor d_a, so new legal actions can be scored without resizing the head.

O2 DESCRIPTOR QUALITY / GROUNDING GAP. Zero-shot action selection only works if descriptors encode features predictive of in-environment effects. MITRE ATT&CK text is useful but noisy, abstract, versioned, and not always aligned with CybORG/CAGE transition dynamics. Naive text embeddings may cluster by prose style or technique ID rather than operational effect. SIGNAL reduces this risk by combining ATT&CK text with structured fields for verb, host/service, privilege, preconditions, and expected effects, then testing with descriptor dropout and held-out-technique splits to detect shortcut learning.

O3 LEGALITY / PRECONDITION MASKING UNDER OPEN CATALOG. In CybORG/CAGE, action legality depends on dynamic host, service, compromise, privilege, and availability state. When the catalog expands, a descriptor scorer may assign high logits to novel actions that are impossible or harmful in the current state. Naively ranking all descriptor actions wastes probability mass or causes invalid transitions. SIGNAL must preserve legal-action masking and extend it with precondition-aware catalog construction at every step, so only currently executable old and novel actions are compared.

O4 NON-STATIONARITY + CREDIT ASSIGNMENT. Adding actions changes the policy distribution, exploration problem, and value estimates. A novel action may be good only in rare states and may never be sampled enough for PPO to assign credit. Naive fine-tuning can either ignore new actions, catastrophically forget old actions, or overfit to expanded-catalog rollouts. SIGNAL addresses this partially, not magically, by training with held-out-technique curricula and descriptor dropout so the scorer learns semantic transfer before test-time expansion, then measuring both zero-shot performance and adaptation sample efficiency.

O5 ADVERSARIAL CO-DEPENDENCE. Blue performance depends on the red policy. If red also receives new TTPs, blue must generalize under an attacker distribution shift, not just under a larger benign action menu. A method that works against B-line may fail against Meander or a held-out exploit family. SIGNAL must therefore be evaluated against multiple CAGE red strategies and withheld red TTP families, with results stratified by attacker type rather than averaged away.

O6 EXACT-COLLAPSE FRAGILITY. Replacing the action head can silently change the baseline even on the original frozen CAGE catalog. Differences in masking order, floating point precision, descriptor lookup, beta routing, or value-head inputs can alter logits and rewards. Naively claiming backward compatibility is not enough. SIGNAL makes exact collapse a first-class engineering constraint: at beta=0, or with one-hot frozen descriptors plus a baseline-initialized linear lookup, it must reproduce baseline logits, sampling distribution, legal-mask behavior, and value inputs with E_collapse <= 1e-6 on fixed seeded rollouts.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

1. ACO/CybORG RL agents and CAGE Challenge systems. CAGE agents, including PPO, hierarchical, recurrent, greedy-decoy, and ensemble blue agents, cover strong fixed-scenario autonomous defense inside CybORG/CAGE [1-5, 13, 14]. They cover none of the core SIGNAL join beyond being the fixed-head baseline environment. They miss C1 because their policies normally end in a fixed softmax/Q-vector over the scenario catalog; miss C2 because action indices are not scored through ATT&CK-grounded structured semantics; miss C3 because held-out CVE/ATT&CK/action-family zero-shot insertion is not the central evaluation; and they do not need C4 because they are the baseline rather than a descriptor-conditioned replacement with an exact-collapse proof.

2. Action-representation RL for large discrete spaces. Dulac-Arnold et al., Chandak et al., and Act2Vec show that action embeddings can help with large or represented discrete action spaces [7-9]. These works partially cover C1 because they weaken the assumption that every action must be an unrelated output index. They miss C2 because their action embeddings are not ATT&CK-grounded cyber descriptors with host, service, privilege, precondition, and expected-effect fields; miss C3 because they do not test zero-shot novel cyber techniques in CybORG/CAGE; and miss C4 because they do not guarantee exact logit-level collapse to an existing certified CAGE policy.

3. Stochastic, varying, and lifelong action-set RL. Boutilier's stochastic action-set MDPs and Chandak et al.'s lifelong changing-action-set work explicitly study changing availability or growing action sets [10, 11]. They cover C1 more directly than standard RL because the action set can vary over time. They miss C2 because action identity is not grounded in MITRE ATT&CK-style cyber semantics; miss C3 because the target is not zero-shot selection of withheld CVE/ATT&CK/action families in ACO; and miss C4 because they do not provide a no-regression collapse path back to a frozen CAGE baseline.

4. Zero-shot, language-conditioned, and tool-use agents. Language-conditioned robotics and tool-use systems such as SayCan, ReAct, and Toolformer show that policies or agents can condition on natural-language or tool descriptions [28-30]. They cover a form of C2 and C3 in other domains: descriptors can guide selection of previously unseen skills or tools. They miss C1 in ACO because they are not solving CybORG/CAGE catalog expansion under legal-action masks and cyber rollout dynamics; miss C4 because they do not exactly reproduce a fixed-head RL baseline at beta=0; and generally miss the ATT&CK/CVE-specific evaluation structure required for cyber defense.

5. Autonomous cyber defense surveys, simulators, and ATT&CK-grounded threat-intelligence systems. CyberBattleSim, FARLAND, intrusion-response RL, and cyber-RL surveys establish the importance of autonomous defense environments and learning-based response [15-18]. ATT&CK and CTI extraction work establish useful cyber semantics for tactics, techniques, and procedures [6, 31]. These cover pieces of C2 or the ACO motivation, but they miss C1 because they do not replace the RL action head with an open-catalog descriptor scorer; miss C3 because they do not evaluate descriptor-only introduction of held-out cyber actions; and miss C4 because they do not prove exact CAGE decision identity on the frozen scenario.

6. Sim-to-real, emulation, and cyber testbeds. DETERlab, Emulab, VINE, SmallWorld, BRAWL, and robotics sim-to-real work address realism, emulation, and the reality gap [19-27]. These works cover the need to test learned behavior outside an ideal simulator, which is adjacent to CybORG's sim-to-emulation motivation [1]. They miss C1 because the central issue is not open action-catalog expansion; miss C2 because they do not score actions through ATT&CK-grounded descriptors; miss C3 because they do not define zero-shot novel-technique action selection; and miss C4 because they do not provide exact collapse to a fixed CAGE policy.

The unclaimed contribution is the C1-C4 join: open-catalog ACO, ATT&CK-grounded action descriptors, zero-shot novel-technique evaluation, and exact collapse to the original CAGE baseline. The conceptual barrier is that prior ACO work inherited the fixed-index action-space framing from standard deep RL. SIGNAL's missing move is to treat the cyber action descriptor, not the catalog index, as the object being scored, while retaining a provable no-regression default.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

METHOD. SIGNAL keeps the CybORG/CAGE observation encoder, PPO objective, legal-action masking, rollout machinery, value head path, and baseline fixed-head policy path. It replaces only the action head with a descriptor-conditioned scorer:

logit(a) = g(h, d_a)

where h is the state embedding from the existing CAGE PPO observation encoder and d_a is the descriptor for legal action a. The main scorer is a bilinear query-key model: q = W_q h, k_a = W_k d_a, logit(a) = q dot k_a + b(d_a). A secondary implementation uses an MLP over [h; d_a; h*d_a] to test whether nonlinear scoring improves transfer. The value function continues to consume the same state features as the PPO baseline unless explicitly ablated.

Each action descriptor d_a concatenates:
- frozen sentence-transformer embedding of MITRE ATT&CK technique or subtechnique text;
- action verb one-hot, such as Monitor, Analyse, Remove, Restore, DecoyServices, exploit, privilege escalation, lateral movement;
- host role and service features;
- privilege level;
- precondition indicators;
- expected effect indicators, such as removes compromise, restores availability, deploys decoy, detects activity, blocks service, or exploits vulnerability;
- optional technique ID embedding, dropped or masked during anti-shortcut training.

At each environment step, SIGNAL builds a legal-action set A_legal(s) from the current CybORG/CAGE state and the expanded catalog. The mask is applied before sampling or argmax, exactly as in the PPO baseline. New CVEs, ATT&CK subtechniques, hosts, services, or defensive actions enter only by adding descriptors to A_legal(s), not by changing the output dimension.

Training uses PPO on standard CAGE rollouts. Descriptor dropout randomly removes text, IDs, host role, or effect fields so the model cannot rely on a single shortcut. Held-out-technique curricula train on seen families while periodically validating on descriptor-only held-out families. The beta gate controls interpolation between the baseline fixed head and SIGNAL scorer. At beta=0, routing goes through the frozen CAGE K-action head. In the one-hot frozen descriptor mode, a baseline-initialized linear lookup exactly reconstructs the original K logits.

DATASETS / ENVIRONMENTS. The primary environments are CybORG and CAGE Challenge 1, 2, 3, and 4 scenarios. The benchmark is CATALOG-EXPANSION: start with a frozen CAGE catalog K, then introduce K+m actions at test without resizing the policy. Splits include:
- held-out defensive families, such as a DecoyServices type, Restore variant, Remove variant, Analyse variant, or service-specific defense;
- held-out attacker exploit families mapped to MITRE ATT&CK techniques;
- host/service-expansion splits where new host roles or services appear only at test;
- red-strategy splits across B-line, Meander, sleep, and held-out red TTP mixtures.

All actions are mapped to MITRE ATT&CK technique or subtechnique descriptors when applicable, with structured CybORG/CAGE fields filling simulator-specific details. CyberBattleSim is an optional secondary environment to test whether descriptor-conditioned scoring transfers beyond CybORG.

METRICS.
1. Frozen-catalog parity: E_collapse = max absolute logit gap over all legal actions and timesteps on a fixed seeded CAGE rollout, requiring E_collapse <= 1e-6. Also report mean reward parity against the published or reproduced frozen-head CAGE PPO baseline.
2. Zero-shot held-out-technique performance: mean episodic reward, optimal-action selection rate where an oracle labels the best novel action, and regret when held-out actions are introduced descriptor-only at test.
3. Catalog-expansion regret: reward gap between SIGNAL and an oracle PPO retrained with all K+m actions available.
4. Adaptation sample efficiency: environment steps needed for SIGNAL fine-tuning to reach fixed percentages of the retrain oracle, compared with retrain-from-scratch.
5. Robustness across held-out red strategies: reward, availability loss, compromise rate, and regret stratified by red strategy and held-out family.

BASELINES.
B1 frozen-head CAGE PPO: original fixed K-action policy. It cannot select novel actions, so it defines the SLOTBLIND lower bound under catalog expansion.
B2 retrain-from-scratch expanded PPO: trained with all K+m actions from the start. This is the expensive oracle/skyline.
B3 random/uniform novel selector: chooses uniformly among novel legal actions when available, testing whether any gain is just exploration.
B4 nearest-ID heuristic: maps each novel descriptor to the nearest known action descriptor or slot and reuses that known logit.
B5 action-embedding RL without ATT&CK grounding: learned action embeddings in the style of action-embedding RL, but without ATT&CK text or structured cyber semantics, isolating contribution C2.
B6 SIGNAL full: ATT&CK-grounded descriptors, structured features, descriptor dropout, held-out-technique training, open-catalog legal-action scoring, and exact-collapse path.

ABLATIONS.
- text-only descriptors vs structured-only vs text + structured features;
- descriptor dropout on vs off;
- frozen text encoder vs finetuned text encoder;
- technique ID included vs masked;
- collapse gate on vs off;
- held-out-family size sweep, for example 10%, 25%, 50% of technique/action families withheld;
- bilinear scorer vs MLP scorer;
- precondition-aware legal mask vs naive descriptor scoring over all catalog actions;
- train only on fixed catalog vs held-out-technique curriculum.

FALSIFIABLE PREDICTIONS.
P1 Zero-shot recovery. On held-out-technique CATALOG-EXPANSION splits, SIGNAL will recover 50-80% of the reward gap between B1 frozen-head PPO and B2 retrain-oracle PPO without any gradient updates on the novel actions. B1 should recover approximately 0-10% because it cannot select unslotted actions. Refutation: SIGNAL recovers less than 25% of the B1-to-B2 gap or is statistically indistinguishable from B4 nearest-ID on most held-out families.

P2 Frozen-catalog identity. On original CAGE catalogs, SIGNAL in collapse mode will satisfy E_collapse <= 1e-6 and mean reward within one standard error of the frozen-head PPO baseline over matched seeds. Refutation: any CI rollout exceeds E_collapse > 1e-6, or reward shifts significantly under beta=0, showing the method changed the baseline rather than extending it.

P3 ATT&CK grounding matters. Removing ATT&CK text and structured cyber fields, as in B5, will reduce zero-shot optimal-action selection rate by 15-30 percentage points relative to full SIGNAL on held-out ATT&CK-family splits. Refutation: B5 matches SIGNAL within 5 percentage points across families, implying the claimed grounding contribution is unnecessary.

EXACT-COLLAPSE CI GATE. The repository should include a deterministic build test that loads a fixed seeded CybORG/CAGE rollout, records every state, legal-action mask, baseline logit vector, sampled or argmax action distribution, and value-head input, then runs SIGNAL in collapse mode. The test computes:

E_collapse = max_t,a |logit_baseline(t,a) - logit_SIGNAL(t,a)|

over all legal original-catalog actions and timesteps. The build fails if E_collapse > 1e-6, if mask ordering changes, if sampled distributions differ under the same seed, or if value inputs differ. This gate is a reproducibility and safety artifact: SIGNAL is not allowed to claim open-catalog improvement unless it first proves exact identity on frozen CAGE.

RESPONSIBLE AI CONSIDERATIONS. ACO is dual-use, so the proposal is deliberately bounded to defense-oriented research in controlled environments. (1) Scope: SIGNAL is evaluated only inside CybORG/CAGE simulation and emulation; we make no claim of readiness for real-world autonomous cyber action without human oversight. (2) Red-agent role: descriptor-conditioned red agents are used solely as controlled adversaries to harden and stress-test blue defenders, not to automate operational attacks. (3) Action abstraction: all actions remain simulator-abstracted CybORG/CAGE primitives; the work contributes no exploit code, payloads, targeting guidance, or deployment procedures. (4) Data governance: descriptors use only provenance-tracked, licensed MITRE ATT&CK and public technique text; non-public CTI, embargoed CVE detail, and PII are excluded unless explicitly authorized. (5) Human-in-the-loop: high-impact responses such as Restore, quarantine, blocking, or service disruption are gated behind abstention/uncertainty thresholds and human review, so the agent is a decision-support tool rather than a sole arbiter; a novel action that the descriptor scorer ranks confidently but cannot justify should trigger abstention rather than autonomous execution. (6) No-regression safety: the exact-collapse CI gate (E_collapse <= 1e-6) is treated as a first-class safety control, guaranteeing the learning extension cannot silently degrade the certified CAGE baseline. (7, advisory) Reporting: results are disaggregated by held-out ATT&CK family, CVE/TTP family, red strategy, and blue action type, so that silent zero-shot failures are visible rather than averaged away.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] Maxwell Standen, Martin Lucas, David Bowman, Toby J. Richer, Junae Kim, and Damian Marriott. "CybORG: A Gym for the Development of Autonomous Cyber Agents." arXiv:2108.09118, 2021. (Source paper; abstract introduces CybORG as a work-in-progress gym for ACO research with simulation and emulation modes under a common interface.)

[2] cage-challenge. "CAGE Challenge 1." GitHub repository, 2021-2022.

[3] cage-challenge. "CAGE Challenge 2." GitHub repository, 2022.

[4] Defence Science and Technology Group / cage-challenge. "Cyber Autonomy Gym for Experimentation (CAGE) Challenge 3." 2022-2023.

[5] cage-challenge. "TTCP CAGE Challenge 4." GitHub Pages and challenge materials, 2024.

[6] Blake Strom, Andy Applebaum, Katie Miller, Kathryn Nickels, Adam Pennington, and Cody Wolf. "MITRE ATT&CK: Design and Philosophy." The MITRE Corporation, Technical Report, 2018.

[7] Yash Chandak, Georgios Theocharous, James Kostas, Scott M. Jordan, and Philip S. Thomas. "Learning Action Representations for Reinforcement Learning." Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

[8] Gabriel Dulac-Arnold, Richard Evans, Hado van Hasselt, Peter Sunehag, Timothy Lillicrap, Jonathan Hunt, Timothy Mann, Theophane Weber, Thomas Degris, and Ben Coppin. "Deep Reinforcement Learning in Large Discrete Action Spaces." arXiv:1512.07679, 2015.

[9] Guy Tennenholtz and Shie Mannor. "The Natural Language of Actions (Act2Vec)." Proceedings of the 36th International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML), 2019.

[10] Craig Boutilier. "Planning and Learning with Stochastic Action Sets." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2018.

[11] Yash Chandak, Georgios Theocharous, James Kostas, Scott M. Jordan, and Philip S. Thomas. "Lifelong Learning with a Changing Action Set." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence, 2020.

[12] Richard S. Sutton and Andrew G. Barto. "Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction." 2nd edition, MIT Press, 2018.

[13] John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, and Oleg Klimov. "Proximal Policy Optimization Algorithms." arXiv:1707.06347, 2017.

[14] Volodymyr Mnih, Koray Kavukcuoglu, David Silver, Andrei A. Rusu, Joel Veness, Marc G. Bellemare, Alex Graves, Martin Riedmiller, Andreas K. Fidjeland, Georg Ostrovski, et al. "Human-level Control through Deep Reinforcement Learning." Nature, 518, 529-533, 2015.

[15] Thanh Thi Nguyen and Vijay Janapa Reddi. "Deep Reinforcement Learning for Cyber Security: A Survey." IEEE Communications Surveys and Tutorials, 24(2), 1348-1383, 2022.

[16] Kim Hammar and Rolf Stadler. "Finding Effective Security Strategies through Reinforcement Learning and Self-Play." 16th International Conference on Network and Service Management (CNSM), 2020.

[17] Microsoft. "CyberBattleSim: An Experimentation and Research Platform to Investigate the Interaction of Automated Agents in an Abstract Simulated Network Environment." GitHub repository and project documentation, 2021.

[18] Andres Molina-Markham, Cory Miniter, Becky Powell, and Ahmad Ridley. "Network Defense is Not a Game: Impact of Network Attacker Modeling and Defending Strategies on Intrusion Detection." arXiv:2104.10262, 2021.

[19] Amrin Maria Khan Adawadkar and Nilima Kulkarni. "Cyber-security and Reinforcement Learning: A Brief Survey." Engineering Applications of Artificial Intelligence, 114, 105116, 2022.

[20] Julian Ibarz, Jie Tan, Chelsea Finn, Mrinal Kalakrishnan, Peter Pastor, and Sergey Levine. "How to Train Your Robot with Deep Reinforcement Learning: Lessons We Have Learned." The International Journal of Robotics Research, 40(4-5), 698-721, 2021.

[21] Jie Tan, Tingnan Zhang, Erwin Coumans, Atil Iscen, Yunfei Bai, Danijar Hafner, Steven Bohez, and Vincent Vanhoucke. "Sim-to-Real: Learning Agile Locomotion for Quadruped Robots." Robotics: Science and Systems, 2018.

[22] Josh Tobin, Rachel Fong, Alex Ray, Jonas Schneider, Wojciech Zaremba, and Pieter Abbeel. "Domain Randomization for Transferring Deep Neural Networks from Simulation to the Real World." IEEE/RSJ International Conference on Intelligent Robots and Systems (IROS), 2017.

[23] Jelena Mirkovic, Terry V. Benzel, Ted Faber, Robert Braden, John T. Wroclawski, and Stephen Schwab. "The DETER Project: Advancing the Science of Cyber Security Experimentation and Test." IEEE International Conference on Technologies for Homeland Security, 2010.

[24] Leigh Stoller, Robert Ricci, Micah Hibler, Chad Barb, and Jay Lepreau. "Emulab: A Testbed Facility Supporting Experimentation with Large-Scale Networks." USENIX Annual Technical Conference, 2008.

[25] Brian E. Eskridge. "VINE: Virtual Network in a Host for Cyber Defense Exercise." 2015.

[26] Alfonso Furfaro, Rosario Culmone, Pasquale De Meo, Giacomo Fiumara, Alfredo Garro, Oreste Lupia, and Giovanni Mastroianni. "SmallWorld: A Framework for Modeling, Analysis and Simulation of Cyber-Physical Systems Security." Future Generation Computer Systems, 78, 795-807, 2018.
